data.title: Lincoln Electric Holdings: Shares Look Expensive As Earnings Near
data.description:
Well over two years ago, in early January of 2023, I wrote an article about Lincoln Electric Holdings (NASDAQ:LECO). At that time, I acknowledged that the firm had done really well leading up to that point. Shares had been on the rise as fundamentals improved. This was great to see. Fortunately, I was not exactly sold on the business. In fact, because of how shares were priced, I took a more neutral stance on the company. At the end of the day, I ended up calling it a "Hold" candidate as opposed to the "Buy" I would have ideally liked it to have been. Whenever I rate a company a "Hold," I am making the claim that I expect shares to perform more or less similarly to the broader market for the foreseeable future. And sure enough, that's pretty much what happened, with shares up 69.7% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 75.8%.

Fast forward to today, and I think it's important to point out that financial performance has been mixed. After reporting a decline in revenue from 2023 to 2024, sales are inching higher. Profitability is up, but two of the three cash flow metrics for the business have worsened. What's more, shares of the company are trading at levels that I would consider to be quite elevated. On a relative basis, the stock is actually a bit cheaper. But that does not do enough in my mind to counteract how pricey shares are on an absolute basis. I wouldn't go so far as to say that it's time to downgrade the company. But I do think caution is warranted.

Of course, I could end up being wrong. We will soon see. After all, before the market opens on Oct. 30, management is expected to announce financial results for the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Revenue and earnings per share are both forecasted to rise nicely. If this does come to fruition, and if we see signs that even more growth is around the corner, I could end up turning bullish. But it would take a heck of a lot of a change in expectations for the future to justify that kind of optimism in light of how the company is valued.

Shares Are A Bit Lofty

Given the amount of time that has passed since my last article, I think it would be appropriate to talk a bit about what Lincoln Electric Holdings is and what it does. According to management, the company is the world leader in the production and sale of arc welding solutions, automated joining, assembly and cutting systems, plasma and oxy-fuel cutting equipment, and more. It even has a major global leading position in the production and sale of brazing and soldering alloys.

These offerings are made available not only throughout the United States, but across the globe. Companies that operate in the energy sector, general fabrication space, heavy industrial markets like shipbuilding, the automotive and transportation space, and the construction and infrastructure industries, all buy the goods that the company provides. In essence, you could say that the entire industrial economy functions at least in part because of what Lincoln Electric Holdings specializes in.

Over the last few years, management has had some lumpy financial results. From 2022 to 2023, for instance, revenue expanded nicely from $3.76 billion to $4.19 billion. But then, in 2024, there was a drop to $4.01 billion. According to management, this decline in revenue was attributable to a $301.2 million hit coming from a reduction in volumes. The picture would have been worse had it not been for a $102.8 million benefit that came from companies that it acquired and had it not been for $30.4 million of additional revenue that the company achieved because of price increases. Management blamed the decline in volume on soft demand across all of its operating segments. Honestly, that's not surprising to me. With high-interest rates aimed at combating inflationary pressures, and now tariffs thrown into the mix, I believe that market weakness is not shocking to see.

As revenue dropped from 2023 to 2024, profitability followed suit. Net income contracted from $545.2 million to $466.1 million. Other profitability metrics for the company also worsened for the most part. As an example, operating cash flow dropped from $667.5 million to $599 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $588.4 million to $556 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $789.5 million to $800.5 million. That was the only bright spot during that time.

For the current fiscal year, we have seen mixed results as well, but for the most part, things are going a bit better than they went last year. Revenue for the first half of 2025, for instance, came in at $2.09 billion. That's an increase of 4.5% compared to the $2 billion the company reported the previous year. Based on the data provided, this increase in revenue was thanks to price increases amounting to $78.6 million and acquisitions that added $78.7 million to the top line for the company. Unfortunately, all of this progress was offset to some extent by lower volumes that negatively affected sales by $61.4 million. Once again, end market demand appears to be weak here. But even in light of that, management can achieve growth by purchasing other firms and hiking prices on customers.

Profitability for the business grew during this time as well, with net income shooting up from $225.1 million to $261.9 million. The rise in revenue brought with it an increase in gross profit. But in addition to this, the firm saw its selling, general, and administrative costs remain virtually flat, actually falling from 20.33% of sales to 19.47%. In addition to this, the company saw a reduction in restructuring costs from $31.1 million last year to only $6.4 million this year. That certainly did a lot of heavy lifting on the bottom line. Operating cash flow also grew, expanding from $304 million to $329.5 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a drop from $307.9 million to $295.9 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company contracted from $398.2 million to $390.9 million.

Long term, I suspect that Lincoln Electric Holdings will do fine. The company is currently the largest player in the $23 billion to $25 billion global arc welding market. This is a space that is growing at a low-single-digit rate on an organic basis. But considering that it is one of only three global providers to offer customers with complete end-to-end solutions in this space, and considering that many of the other players in the industry are considerably smaller than it is, there is the opportunity to continue growing by acquisitions.

One particularly interesting niche of this market that I find myself excited about would be automation. Management believes that labor shortages, the need for improved productivity and operational efficiency, onshoring and reshoring associated with hostile trade policies, and improvements in technology, will lead to greater investments in automation by its customers. In fact, management has built an entire automation team of around 2,500 individuals, and between 29 automation facilities spread across 12 different countries, it has about 2.6 million square feet of space at its disposal. Last year alone, the firm generated about $911 million in revenue from this market. And with it expected to continue growing at a high-single-digit rate in the long run, there is plenty of upside. In fact, for this year alone, the company is hoping to capture about $1 billion in revenue from this industry. There are, naturally, other opportunities as well, such as clean technology growth, that should bode well for shareholders eventually.

Even though management is focused on growth initiatives, it's not against returning capital to shareholders as well. From 2020 through this year, the company has adopted a strategy of returning about 50% of its available capital to shareholders annually. The other 50% is dedicated toward internal capital expenditures and acquisition opportunities. This provides a nice balance for investors who want a little bit of both. Although personally, I would prefer that management focus on growth above all else, especially considering how pricey shares are.

In the chart above, you can see how the company is valued using historical results from 2024 and estimates for 2025. These are multiples that, quite honestly, I consider to be on the pricey side of the spectrum. Normally, I would entertain a downgrade here. But relative to other similar firms, shares are surprisingly not expensive. In the table below, I stacked it up against five other similar enterprises. While on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, it was the second most expensive of the group; it was the cheapest relative to earnings and the second cheapest on an EV-to-EBITDA basis.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Lincoln Electric Holdings 24.1 24.4 17.8
Nordson Corporation (NDSN) 29.4 21.9 18.2
IDEX Corporation (IEX) 27.0 19.6 15.6
ITT Inc. (ITT) 28.4 24.0 19.1
Graco (GGG) 30.1 21.7 20.0
RBC Bearings (RBC) 47.1 36.6 25.4

Even though I'm not downgrading the company at this time, a lot can change based on new data that comes in. In the table below, you can see historical results for the third quarter of the 2024 fiscal year compared to analysts' estimates for the third quarter of this year. As I mentioned at the start of this article, management will be reporting results for that quarter before the market opens on Oct. 30. Leading up to that point, analysts do anticipate revenue rising from $983.8 million last year to $1.05 billion this year. They also expect earnings per share to rise from $1.77 to $2.43, which would take net profits up from $100.8 million to $136 million. In all likelihood, if analysts are correct about these metrics, cash flows will improve as well. But unless the company can really surprise me, upgrading it is off the table.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I still believe that Lincoln Electric Holdings is an interesting business. But I don't think it is a compelling opportunity right now. Shares are pricey on an absolute basis, and financial performance has been mixed. I expect the firm to continue growing. But because of how the stock is priced, an upgrade is almost certainly not happening. And while an upgrade might be possible if future data comes in strong, I would say that there is a higher probability of a downgrade than there is an upgrade.

data.title: AEye: A Potential Asymmetric Upside, Q3 2025 Will Define Its Fate
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Denis Doroshenko as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

AEye’s (LIDR) revenue growth remains uncertain, and its current rate of cash burn suggests further share price downside over the next 12 months. Indeed, revenue from partnerships could face delays or cancellations, and an annual 2025 cash burn of $27–29 million (previously communicated by the management and reaffirmed in the Q2 2025 report) with only $19.2 million in cash on hand as of Q2 2025 implies a c.8-month runway without any additional capital raise. As fundamentals remain fragile and AEye cannot raise capital without any further dilution, I see a potential further downside for the share price. I would stay on the sidelines for now and wait for positive signs in the Q3 2025 results before reassessing the stock.

What does the Company do?

AEye (Nasdaq: LIDR) develops software-defined Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) systems designed to make roads, cities, and vehicles safer and smarter. Its technology supports driver-assistance features, autonomous mobility, intelligent infrastructure, security, and logistics. The company's flagship sensor, Apollo, is known for its compact size and its ability to spot objects from as far as one kilometer away. In fact, the 4Sight platform is using AI and software algorithms to decide where to focus its laser—exactly like a human eye would adjust its focus.

Beyond individual sensors, AEye also provides an integrated solution called OPTIS—a full platform that also interprets the data and helps systems respond instantly to what’s happening in real time.

Growing Visibility but an Uncertain Revenue Conversion

To give more perspective, looking at the Company 10-K reports on an annual basis from 2022 to 2024, the prototype sales trend is on a sharp decline, as per the table below. Total revenue declined 60% in 2023 and 86% in 2024.

However, I believe AEye has strong momentum, signing partnerships very recently. Earlier this month, AEye began delivering its Apollo sensor system to a major U.S. defense contractor. The technology will be used in both helicopters and drones, marking a possible new chapter for the Company. The project highlights how AEye’s LiDAR systems can move beyond automotive and infrastructure. And we know that in selective verticals such as infrastructure, security, and defense, early entrants can lock in customers.

Earlier in July, the company's Apollo LiDAR system was integrated into Nvidia’s DRIVE AGX platform, an AI-based system used for autonomous and advanced driver-assistance applications. This partnership opens significant commercial opportunities for AEye by giving the company direct access to Nvidia’s global network of automakers.

Yet, revenue from partnerships remains uncertain and could be subject to delays or cancellations. In my opinion, the likelihood of contracts generating concrete revenue at this stage is quite speculative.

Weak Liquidity Position Requires Further Capital and Risks Diluting Shareholders

The company is software-driven; the product is well differentiated on the market; the business is 80% recurring with a licensing/royalty model and high margins. Such models can definitely justify higher multiples if the Company manages to scale.

On the revenue side, AEye runs a licensing business model that has strong potential. Revenue sources are quite diversified across sales to OEMs and infrastructure and defense contracts. The company engaged with more than 100 potential customers and has signed 6 contracts in 2025. Most importantly, a leading OEM signed for a potential $30 million in revenue expected to begin generating revenue this year.

On the cost side, we can see from the table below that AEye has been aggressively cutting the S&M and SG&A costs over the years, reducing total operating expenses by 2.6x from 2022 to 2024. However, on a relative basis against the revenue, the operating costs are still extremely high (179x the 2024 revenue), questioning the sustainability of the operations going forward.

Looking at the balance sheet, an annual 2025 cash burn of $27–29 million and only $19.2 million in cash on hand as of Q2 2025 implies a c.8-month runway without any additional capital raise. Such a weak liquidity position will require capital injection this year, which suggests further shareholder dilution down the road. At this stage, it is unclear to me who will provide AEye with new money.

The Market May Adjust The Multiple Downward

In my view, there is a good chance that AEye's cash flow will remain negative over the next couple of years. Therefore, to value the company, I am looking at forward EV/revenue multiples. The TTM multiple is extremely high due to low TTM sales and so is not meaningful.

For valuation purposes, I assume $40 million in revenue in 2025, considering the new $30 million OEM contract that slips to 2026 and only partial ramp from 6 signed contracts, where defense/infra adds ~$5 million. In such a case, the 2025 forward EV/revenue would be around 2.7x ($107.7 million in EV / $40 million in sales). After the capital raise, the effect on the EV (should be neutral in theory, as the increase in equity is balanced by the increase in cash) will largely depend on the market reaction to the news. But here, the capital raise is required for survival, so I expect the impact of the capital increase to be neutral to negative on the enterprise value. Therefore, I believe the post-equity raise 2025 forward EV/revenue should be lower than 2.7x and would signal a distress, especially if peer group multiples are revised upwards. As fundamentals remain fragile and AEye cannot raise capital without any further dilution, I see it as a potential downside for the share price.

A Highly Competitive LiDAR Market Further Puts Pressure

The LiDAR market remains highly competitive, with regulatory and technological threats. The Chinese automotive market has moved to mass adoption of LiDAR, and the global market TAM is set to reach $3.5 billion by 2032, even as Western OEMs are lagging.

Alternative technologies, such as camera + AI, might reduce the need for expensive LiDARs. New sensing approaches (solid-state, no moving parts, unconventional sensors) could undercut AEye’s potential. Competitors such as Luminar (LAZR), Innoviz (INVZ), Ouster (OUST), and Aeva (AEVA) are rapidly securing automotive and mobility contracts. Luminar’s systems are already in production with Volvo and Mercedes-Benz, while Innoviz supplies BMW and has a multi-billion-dollar program with the Volkswagen Group. Aeva’s partnership with Daimler Truck North America represents one of the largest commercial lidar programs to date. It feels like AEye is more of a Tier-2 player for now unless it converts its partnerships into revenue quickly.

The 12-Month Share Price Confirms Fundamental Weaknesses

AEye's share price looks highly speculative; it failed to break out of the bearish trend line in July above the $5.5 level on the Nvidia partnership news. For now, the short interest is 9.5%, keeping the downtrend trend intact.

Performance Is Not Sustainable

If AEye succeeds in building a strong pipeline and achieving scale, its high-margin licensing model could make it a very appealing growth story for patient investors. But for now, the company still has to prove it can execute. With modest revenue and ongoing cash burn, it is difficult to take a fully bullish stance just yet. Still, its integration with Nvidia’s DRIVE platform, the steady expansion of partnerships, and the momentum in its industrial business suggest to me that the company might be nearing a turning point.

At present, performance is not sustainable without meaningful contract wins converting into revenue. I will rate the stock a Hold for now and will wait for the Q3 early November results and monitor cash burn vs. actual expenses and possible capital raises before I can revisit my investment view on the company.

data.title: GDMN: A Modern Stacked Play On Gold And Miners
data.description:
Gold continues to be the center of attention in a market trading on inflation, real yields and geopolitics. The WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Gold Miners Strategy Fund (BATS:GDMN) is one way to play all that. For those investors who want exposure to gold and the minders themselves in one vehicle, GDMN is a strong way to get it in one ticker.

Strategy Overview

GDMN was launched in late 2021 and it’s actively managed, designed to offer both direct and indirect exposure to gold. The fund's portfolio contains a variety of worldwide gold mining stocks, including over-the-counter ones. This structure enables investors to gain the combined effect of physical gold and miners in one ETF—without having to purchase both individually on their own or add leverage.

WisdomTree has dubbed this a “capital-efficient” strategy: The fund will invest in gold miners with approximately 90% of assets, and U.S.-listed gold futures with about 90% of notional exposure. The fund basically stacks the two. That of course multiplies gains and losses. So GDMN is essentially a leveraged mix of a gold ETF and a miners ETF.

The concept dovetails nicely with the current macro backdrop. Higher levels of sustained inflation and choppy market swings have sparked an increased need for a store of value by investors and this has once again brought gold to their attention. And, for now, gold miners are finally reaping some benefits from stronger metal prices and disciplined capital spending after years of underperformance. GDMN is in the sweet spot of these two trends: it seeks to capture the potential upside from both themes with a single allocation.

Portfolio Construction

GDMN owns approximately 50 of the largest gold miners in the world and layers on top the futures contract for gold to reflect exposure to the metal itself. The equity sleeve targets firms generating at least half their revenue from gold mining. Some well known names rule the roost in the portfolio; Newmont, Agnico-Eagle, Barrick, AngloGold Ashanti, Gold Fields and Kinross. Combined, the top 10 holdings account for just over half of the fund’s total exposure, providing investors diversified access to the largest and most liquid mining companies.

Geography-wise, the portfolio is heavily tilted towards Canada and the U.S., but it also holds meaningful positions in South Africa, Australia and the U.K. This blend of the international and emerging market arenas offers access to mature stable operators with steady revenues, yet brings with it emerging-market exposure for added potential growth.

The other half of GDMN is in its futures overlay. The fund takes long positions in COMEX gold contracts to achieve a similar level of performance as the spot price of gold. Futures are insured by short-term Treasury collateral, and there is no margin or rolling contracts for investors to deal with. GDMN directly benefits from those futures when gold prices move higher, and the stocks in the portfolio generally magnify moves in futures due to the operating leverage they have as business operators in the space. If the price of gold falls, losses on futures add to weaker mining stock performance.

Its 0.45% expense ratio, while not dirt cheap for a fund relying on sophisticated derivatives strategies, is reasonable. The double-exposure structure will still result in higher volatility than unlevered peers, but better serves investors with a strong, directional view on gold.

Peer ETFs: Structure & Volatility

There are of course other ways of investing in the space, like the SPDR Gold Trust (GLD), which is backed by physical gold and generally has lower volatility. The VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) consists of mining stocks, which are more volatile than Gold by itself based on operational leverage. Direxion’s NUGT is a 2× miners fund, daily resetting but with much more volatility and which is designed for short-term traders. GDMN sits between these extremes. Due to its blend of exposure to gold miners and gold futures, it has more volatility than either GLD or GDX but less than a daily-reset fund like NUGT.

Leverage Structure

GDMN invests the majority of its assets in gold producers and has substantial notional exposure to U.S. gold futures. This results in a “stacked” configuration – two layers of gold response are effectively delivered simultaneously in one vehicle. This results in increased sensitivity to gold price moves, both via futures and through miner equity exposure.

Though it is not daily reset, the fund’s design (dual exposure) also increases volatility. Leveraged ETFs are more dangerous than plain old funds and not everyone should own them. GDMN is meant as a tactical play for those who are comfortable with short-term risk and sector concentration — it’s not a buy-and-hold core portfolio play in my view.

Conclusion

GDMN is a powerful leveraged way to exert confidence in gold. For investors who believe gold will keep benefiting from worldwide uncertainty or a prospective easing cycle, GDMN is an aggressive way to profit from that view. It isn’t a conservative hedge, or a core portfolio holding — it’s a tactical tool for investors comfortable with leverage and sector concentration. In small quantities, GDMN serves as a complement to a diversified portfolio and increases exposure to one of the world’s oldest hedges in a modern, efficient vehicle.

data.title: Exxon Mobil: Prepare For A Cold Winter
data.description:
XOM Stock: Oil And Natural Gas Could Move In Opposite Directions

I last covered Exxon Mobil Corporation (NYSE:XOM) Aug 26 with an article titled "Exxon Mobil: $100 Oil Price Can Be A 2025 Black Swan". The article analyzed the potential catalysts that could push oil prices higher in the 2H of 2025 and rated XOM as a buy. Since then, a few new catalysts have evolved around XOM. In the rest of this article, I will elaborate on three of them: the oil price change, the potential of an OPEC oil production boost, and the possibility of higher-than-expected demand for natural gas (NG) in the winter of 2025. These developments can produce competing or even conflicting forces on XOM. But in the end, the overall return/risk profile is skewed.

Let me start with the changes in oil prices. Oil prices have decreased quite a bit since my last writing, as you can see from the following chart, and now hover around $62 (vs. a 52-week low of about $59). A few uncertainties could further pressure oil prices. The two most immediate and also consequential ones in my mind are A) the potential of a re-escalation of the trade tension between the U.S. and China, and B) the possibility of further OPEC oil production increases. These factors could keep oil prices pressured, and in turn, pressure XOM's profits and share prices.

However, a competing catalyst has also been developing since my last analysis. According to the latest weather forecast,

"a large portion of the U.S. is expected to face a colder-than-average winter in 2025 and also 2026 with the Northern Plains, Great Lakes, and New England likely seeing the most intense cold and snow, particularly in mid-January and mid-February."

Of course, oil is a key revenue stream for XOM, but it is also a key producer of NG for the U.S. market, and thus investors should not ignore the role of NG, especially given the possibility of a cold winter ahead. Next, I will detail my analysis on natural gas demand and its impact on XOM's profits in the near term (say in the next 4~6 months or so).

XOM Stock: Natural Gas Price, Inventory, And Demand

NG is currently priced slightly below its 5-year historical average. To be more explicit, the next chart displays the Henry Hub Natural Gas Spot Price (I:HHNGSP) between 2021 and now. As you can see, the current NG price of $3.32 is about 10% below the 5-year average of $3.67. Also note that the peak prices observed earlier this year were about $4.6, far above the current level.

But the current NG inventory is quite tight in my view, and a slight demand increase could shift the demand-supply dynamics and trigger a price rally. More specifically, working NG inventory in storage for the lower 48 states was 3,618 billion cubic feet (Bcf) according to the latest EIA data as of the week of Oct 3. The current NG underground storage is about 4.5% above the 5-year historical averages (or about 156 Bcf in absolute terms). It is also about 0.6% higher than the same period last year (or by about 22 Bcf in absolute terms).

However, readers need to bear in mind that the A) the baseline NG demand could have increased already over the past 5 years due to economic development, and B) a surplus of 22 Bcf (vs. last year's level) or 156 Bcf (vs. the 5-year average) is a quite small buffer compared to fluctuations in demand. To better contextualize things, NG consumption in the residential sector alone was on average 12~13 Bcf PER DAY in recent years. Moreover, the consumption is quite sensitive to weather conditions and could more than double the annual average in colder months.

XOM Stock's Earnings: Impact From Natural Gas

With the bigger picture above, the table below shows my back-of-the-envelope calculation specific for XOM. The analysis involved some units conversion between Bcf mentioned above and MBTU (million British Thermal Units). Readers new to these things could either trust my math or see my earlier article for a detailed explanation so we can stay focused on the key parameters I assumed here. This estimate needs a breakeven price for NG production to start, and I plugged in a breakeven price of $2.80/MBTU taken from this McKinsey report. The 2nd key parameter needed is, of course, XOM's NG production capacity, which was taken to be 8.7 Bcf per day by averaging its throughput in recent years. In a later section, I will explain why this could leave some upside risks. With these parameters, the bottom-line is a change of $0.1/MBTU in NG prices could result in a change of $0.08 in XOM's EPS in my model.

Armed with the above EPS sensitivity analysis with respect to NG price, the next chart is very simple in comparison. This chart simply shows my projection for XOM's stock prices as a function of various NG prices and P/cash ratios. At XOM's current prices of $112, its P/Cash multiple is only about 8.8x on a TTM basis and about 8.7x on an FWD basis. These multiples are towards the lower end of its historical range. Given my above anticipation of higher NG demand in the coming winter, I expect an expansion of its P/cash flow ratio in tandem. As such, I consider the combination of a 10x P/cash multiple and an NG price of $4.5 to $5.0 to be the most likely outcome in the next 4~6 months. Under such a projection, the target price for XOM is in the range of $130 to $135, as highlighted in the table below, pointing to a sizable upside in a relatively short period of time.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

As another reflection of XOM's attractive valuation, its current dividend yield of 3.5% is noticeably above its historical average of 3.39% in the past 3 years, as you can see from the following chart (top panel). As a horizontal comparison, the bottom panel shows its current dividend yield spread (YS) relative to the broader energy sector approximated by the XLE ETF. As seen, the current YS is 0.192%, also above the 3-year average YS of -0.076% by a noticeable margin and indicating attractive valuation relative to the sector too. Finally, most XOM investors must be aware of its dividend champion status, which demonstrates its far stronger financial strength and superior profit consistency compared to the overall sector.

In terms of downside risks, investment in XOM faces a common set of risks generic to the energy sector, which include but are not limited to policy risks, geopolitical conflicts, the secular shift toward carbon-free sources, and so on. In this article, I will limit myself to the risks that are of more immediate relevance to this analysis. To start, the potential of an OPEC production boost is an immediate uncertainty to my above projections. OPEC discussions are more centered around oil production, which often produces natural gas as a by-product. Thus, OPEC's decision about their production can influence not only oil prices (in a direct and linear way) but also NG prices too (in a more indirect way). As a more uncertain element, the Russian/Ukraine conflict could come to an end with the diplomatic efforts from the Trump administration and its recent progress toward resolving other regional conflicts (such as the Gaza peace). Russia is a key producer of NG and oil in the global market, and the outcome and timeline of this conflict could dramatically alter the supply-demand dynamics I anticipated above.

To close, this XOM reexamination focused on 3 new developments: oil price change, OPEC production, and weather conditions. Admittedly, these developments pose competing or even conflicting forces on XOM stock. But my assessment is that the current market is overly pessimistic and underestimates the potency of positive catalysts. An immediate and important one in my view is a colder-than-expected winter, which could trigger both a profit boost and valuation expansion for XOM in the near term.

data.title: TSMC Continues To Outperform, Powered By The AI Boom
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM) (OTC:TSMWF) delivered another excellent earnings report, which is something that the company tends to do consistently.

The numbers for the quarter are ahead of expectations, management raised guidance, and capital spending is also increasing. This has positive implications for both the investment thesis in TSM and the demand outlook in the AI boom at a general level.

The Numbers

Total revenue during the third quarter of 2025 reached $33.1 billion, versus $31.5 billion projected by Wall Street. It is important to keep in mind that TSM reports revenue numbers every month, which provides more visibility to analysts. This means that Wall Street estimates are usually not too far away from the reported number.

The 5.1% revenue outperformance this quarter is quite high by TSM's historical standards, and it is even the biggest revenue beat that the company has produced over the past several years. This is not easy to do, since TSM is facing a high bar due to AI-powered revenue acceleration in recent quarters.

Advanced nodes, meaning seven nanometers and below, accounted for 74% of wafer revenue during the quarter. This has positive implications in terms of both competitive positioning and pricing power for TSM.

I was expecting a slight contraction in gross profit margins due to the negative impact of lower productivity in overseas fabs. However, TSM announced a 0.9% increase in gross profit margin sequentially. This is because cost improvements and higher capacity utilization are more than offsetting the negative impact of dilution from overseas fabs and currency headwinds.

Higher than anticipated margins speak well about both pricing and underlying demand trends for the leading semiconductor manufacturer.

For the second half of 2025, management now expects gross margin dilution from overseas fabs to be closer to 2% than the prior range of 2% to 3%. Again, this is also driven by increased capacity utilization and cost efficiencies.

Management has a clear focus on sustaining profitability at scale. From the conference call:

Overall, with our fundamental competitive advantages of manufacturing technology leadership and a large-scale production base, we expect TSMC to be the most efficient and cost-effective manufacturer in every region that we operate.

The company also raised the low end of its CapEx guidance to $40-$42 billion versus a prior range of $38-$42 billion.

The management team naturally faced some questions during the conference call about the timing of CapEx versus demand opportunities, and TSM leadership clarified that they are only increasing CapEx based on current demand flows and not anticipating future opportunities that may or may not materialize. This should help alleviate investors' concerns about potential overspending during the boom phase of the product cycle.

TSM expects revenue growth this year to be between 30% and 40%, which is higher than the previously projected 30%. Fourth-quarter revenue guidance stands at $32.8 billion, versus $32 billion expected by analysts. Gross margin guidance for the next quarter is 60% versus 57% projected by Wall Street.

Management specifically said in the conference call that TSM has not observed any change in customer behavior so far based on tariff policies. However, the company understands that this can be a source of uncertainty going forward, so TSM plans to be prudent when it comes to business planning for 2026.

The Implications

The earnings report from TSM is above expectations across the board. Management is usually conservative with guidance, so it is excellent news to see TSM raising guidance on both revenue and profitability.

TSM is the leading manufacturer for top tech companies, including names like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), and Apple Inc. (AAPL), among many others. If TSM is doing so well, it means the AI boom is still strong and the tech sector continues to thrive.

Management confirmed that AI demand is still exceeding expectations during the conference call.

Recent developments in AI market continue to be very positive. The explosive growth in token volume demonstrate the increasing consumer AI model adoption which means more and more computation is needed, leading to more leading-edge silicon demand.

Valuation And Timing

TSM stock has gained nearly 54% year to date, so investors may consider that it is too late to buy the stock at current prices. However, valuation is about comparing the stock price versus fundamental metrics of value and not versus prior prices. Investment decisions should be made by looking through the windshield as opposed to the rearview mirror.

TSM is currently trading at a forward P/E ratio of 26.5 times earnings projections for 2026 and 21.6 times earnings estimates for 2027.

Fiscal Period Ending EPS Estimate YoY Growth Forward P/E
Dec 2025 9.83 39.58% 31.01
Dec 2026 11.49 16.93% 26.52
Dec 2027 14.13 22.94% 21.57

Data Source: Seeking Alpha Premium

This valuation is not too expensive for a market leader with undisputed competitive strengths due to scale advantages, generating rock-solid profitability, and outperforming expectations broadly.

Besides, valuation is always a dynamic number. Wall Street analysts have been consistently running from behind and increasing their earnings projections for TSM as the company continues to beat expectations time and again.

After another brilliant quarter from TSM in Q3, I can only expect more upward revisions to earnings in the days ahead, meaning that the stock is cheaper than current valuation metrics indicate.

TSM stock is in a clean and vigorous uptrend. I wouldn't chase the stock with a big position at all-time highs, but any pullbacks toward $280 could be a good entry point.

Risk And Reward Going Forward

No investment is immune to risks. Investors in TSM need to be willing to accept short-term price volatility, depending on how geopolitical tensions with China evolve. Furthermore, if we see an increased risk of military conflict over Taiwan, that would be a reason to reassess the investment thesis in TSM to a large degree and probably to even consider closing the position.

Semiconductor demand is naturally cyclical, even if AI has shifted the demand curve materially. At some point in the future, demand could slow down more than anticipated, producing some turbulence for TSM stock.

There is no sign of such a slowdown taking place anytime soon. On the contrary, both numbers for the quarter and forward-looking guidance are outstanding. However, it remains a significant risk factor to be aware of in the future.

This being acknowledged, TSM is an excellent company at the epicenter of the AI boom; it has a key strategic place due to its undisputed market leadership in semiconductor manufacturing, the business is firing on all cylinders, and profitability continues to exceed expectations. The stock is not too cheap, but it is fairly valued considering the quality of the business. TSM looks well-positioned to continue outperforming expectations going forward.

data.title: High-Beta Stocks Take The Lead For Equity Risk Factors In 2025
data.description:
A handful of equity risk factors are outperforming the broad stock market this year, and within the elite winner’s circle, so-called high-beta shares are leading the field, based on a set of ETFs through Wednesday’s close (Oct. 15).

The Invesco S&P 500 High Beta ETF (SPHB) is up a sizzling 27.3% year to date. The strong run this year puts the fund well ahead of the stock market overall, which has rallied 14.5% via the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

SPHB targets the stocks in the S&P 500 with the highest sensitivity to market movements, or beta, over the past 12 months.

The ETF is also outperforming the rest of the factor field. It’s closest competitors for year-to-date results: the momentum factor (MTUM) and micro-cap stocks (IWC), both of which are also beating the broad market in 2025. The fund’s current top-3 holdings - a trio of tech firms - account for roughly 5.9% of the portfolio: Micro Technology (MU), Tesla (TSLA) and Intel (INTC), according to Morningstar.com.

Earlier in the year, the high beta strategy (SPHB) had been trailing equities overall (SPY) by a wide margin. But SPHB’s recovery has accelerated in recent months and has become the performance leader as the trading year heads into 2025’s final 2-1/2-month stretch.

All the equity risk factors are posting gains year to date, but results vary widely. The weakest performer: small-cap value stocks (IJS), which is up a tepid 3.5% this year.

data.title: TSMC: Unprecedented Growth Outlook For A Company Of This Scale
data.description:
Performance assessment

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM) (OTC:TSMWF) has outperformed the market since my last update on the stock, where I took some profits off the table:

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company is a long name. So that's why this is often referred to as TSMC. I will use the same acronym in this article.

Thesis

I have recently added to my position in TSM stock, as I am getting very bullish:

  1. The outlook is very bright as AI related demand is even stronger than expected just a quarter ago.
  2. There are many favorable margin developments.
  3. I like TSM's valuations here.
  4. There is no reason to fight a healthy, clear uptrend.

  5. TSM's increased investments in the U.S. mitigate tariff related risks.

The outlook is very bright, as AI-related demand is even stronger than expected just a quarter ago

The growth prospects for TSMC are tremendously strong. That's not just my opinion. This is what the CEO is telling us very directly:

…the AI demand actually continue to be very strong, it's more --more stronger than we thought 3 months ago, okay? So in today's situation, we have talked to customers and then we talk to customers' customer. So the CAGR [5-year growth CAGR] we -- previously we announced is about mid-40s. It's still -- it's a little bit better than that. We will update you probably in beginning of next year. So we have a more clear picture. Today, the number are insane.

- CEO C.C. Wei in the Q3 FY25 earnings call, Author's emphasis.

For me, the big highlight in that comment that gives me greater conviction for a longer-term bullish view on the stock is the fact that the 5-year growth outlook for AI chip sales is expected to be better than an already high mid-40s % growth rate. This is quite remarkable for a company with $119 billion in annual revenues (and $68 billion in high-performance computing revenues, which may correlate with AI chip sales, although I suspect some automotive chip sales should also be included in this category)!

Unsurprisingly, the high performance computing segment is the key growth contributor here. TSMC leads the market in terms of the most advanced chips and that is being reflected via a steady, relentless increase in market share (see chart above). The demand for AI compute as measured in tokens is increasing at a very fast clip at more than 30% YoY by some industry measures. And TSMC's CEO highlighted how the company's continuous innovation in next-generation processing nodes and customers' rapid uptake of that is enabling this high token usage growth:

… And let me tell you that, first, our technology continue to improve. And so our customer moving from one node to the next node so that they can handle much more tokens number in their basic fundamental calculation. So that's one thing. We progressed very well for one node into the other node, and our customer working with TSMC to continuously to improve their performance. And that's why when we say that we have about 40%, 45%, CAGR, then the token number are exponentially increased because of our customer and TSMC's technology combined that can handle much more or much efficient than before.

- CEO C.C. Wei in the Q3 FY25 earnings call, Author's emphasis.

Another takeaway that I think may miss the headlines due to the automotive segment's smaller 5% revenue mix is the fact that this segment has been seeing accelerated growth for the last year and a bit to now grow the second-fastest out of all segments. I believe this may give us clues on Tesla's (TSLA) robotaxi ramp as that company is a top 10 customer of TSMC.

This narrative is being reflected in an upgraded growth guidance outlook as well as FY25 revenues were bumped up to reflect ~35% YoY growth, up from the earlier 30% YoY:

This also led to a larger than usual beat on revenue guidance, which suggests that the expectations may still not be priced in completely in the stock, making a case for upside:

There are many favorable margin developments

The scale up of TSMC's 3nm (N3) node is expected to result in a marginal improvement in yields and gross margins. Now, some of this benefit will be offset by the fact that the 2nm (N2) node will see some initial gross margin dilution as that ramps up in 2026. And the good news is that longer term, the N2 node's profitability is expected to be even better than that of the N3:

N2's structural profitability is better than the N3, okay?

- CFO Jen-Chau Huang in the Q3 FY25 earnings call.

Another favorable development is that the margin dilution from overseas fabs is expected to be lower due to scale benefits and cost improvement efforts:

While the cost of overseas fabs remain higher, thanks to the company's overall larger scale, we now expect the gross margin dilution from the ramp-up of our overseas fabs to be closer to 2% in the second half of 2025. For the full year 2025, we now expect it to be between 1% to 2% as compared to 2% to 3% previously.

- CFO Jen-Chau Huang in the Q3 FY25 earnings call, Author's emphasis.

These profitability boosts are leading to meaningful positive beats on gross margin guidance as well:

Overall, this makes me bullish on TSMC's gross margin profile long term. I think we can see it breach 60% next year (after the N2 ramp) and reach at least 65%+ after the current scale up cycle is complete:

I like TSM's valuations here

TSM stock is trading at a 1-yr fwd P/E of 22.6x, which corresponds to a 6.3% discount to the semiconductor comps' median multiple of 24.1x.

Another encouraging sign is that the fundamental outlook in the company as reflected in the earnings expectations index is increasing whilst the valuation multiple remains flat. This indicates an opportunity to buy a better company at the same price:

So I like find TSM's valuations rather attractive, especially given the bright outlook ahead. I think there is a clear mispricing as I cannot fathom how a company with such bright revenue growth and margin expansion prospects with a monopoly position in the highest performing chips that fuel the digital world today, can trade at a mere 22.6x 1-yr fwd P/E, lower than even the broader S&P 500's (SPY, SPX) 22.8x.

There is no reason to fight a healthy, clear uptrend

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of TSM vs SP500

TSM is in a healthy, clear uptrend vs. the S&P 500. Why fight the trend? I see no good reason to do so.

TSMC's increased investments in the U.S. mitigate tariff related risks

Whilst the U.S.-China trade war can dampen demand from Chinese customers for TSMC's chips, I suspect that the impact may be mitigated as TSMC's revenue mix profile clearly shows greater alignment toward the U.S. and the broader world:

TSMC is also increasing investments in the U.S. too as it plans to acquire a second large piece of land in Arizona to create another giga fab cluster:

In addition, we are preparing to upgrade our technologies faster to -- and to a more advanced process technologies in Arizona, given the strong AI-related demand from our customers. Furthermore, we are close to securing a second large piece of land nearby to support our current expansion plans and provide more flexibility in response to the very strong multiyear AI-related demand. Our plan will enable TSMC to scale up through an independent giga fab cluster in Arizona to support the needs of our leading-edge customers in smartphone, AI and HPC applications.

- CEO C.C. Wei in the Q3 FY25 earnings call, Author's emphasis.

Hence, I believe the impact of geopolitical, tariff and export control risk is mitigated by these actions.

Takeaway & Positioning

TSMC has annual revenues of $119 billion. Yet, even at that scale, management is clearly indicating a ~45% CAGR growth for the next 5 years. This is unprecedented. And a very strong reason to be uber-bullish on the stock, especially since the longer term gross margin outlook is also turning out more favorable.

Despite these tailwinds, TSM stock is still trading at a discount to its semiconductor sectors' median 1-yr fwd P/E multiples and below that of the S&P 500's multiple too. This may be because the market is still less confident on the geopolitical and tariff-related risks. However, my analysis of the revenue mix profile and TSMC's investments in the U.S. indicate that this risk is mitigated somewhat. Hence, I really like the stock's valuations here.

Lastly, the technical charts also show a clear bullish uptrend vs the S&P 500. Hence, I am confident that TSMC will continue to outperform.

Rating: 'Strong Outperform/Strong Buy'

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Outperform/Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Outperform/Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis

Market Perform/Hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis

Underperform/Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Underperform/Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: PBW: Understanding The Structure And Suitability Of This Clean Energy ETF
data.description:
About The Invesco WilderHill Clean Energy ETF

The Invesco WilderHill Clean Energy ETF (NYSEARCA:PBW) is an exchange-traded index fund offered by Invesco (IVZ) that aims to track the performance of the WilderHill Clean Energy Index. According to the fund’s website:

The Invesco WilderHill Clean Energy ETF is based on the WilderHill Clean Energy Index. The fund will normally invest at least 90% of its total assets in common stocks that comprise the Index. The Index is composed of stocks of companies that are publicly traded in the United States and engaged in the business of advancement of cleaner energy and conservation. The Fund and the Index are rebalanced and reconstituted quarterly.

PBW launched on March 3, 2005, which makes it one of the first exchange-traded index funds to come out following a 2004 publication entitled “Who Cares Wins,” which is widely credited with popularizing “socially responsible investing” and Environmental, Social, and Governance investment themes. The concept revolves around ranking companies based on their environmental and social impact and then favoring those companies that rank highly in those categories. With that said, the Invesco WilderHill Clean Energy ETF does not, strictly speaking, advertise itself as an ESG fund, nor does the index specifically state that it is weighting companies based on their social impact or governance structure. However, the index’s website does suggest that it is not strictly weighting companies based on market capitalization like most other equity indices. From the website:

We apply qualitative analysis at the Quarterly rebalancings to determine Index securities, sectors and weightings. Criteria include importance of the stock and sector to clean energy, relevance to climate change, pollution prevention, technological significance, intellectual property rights, salience to preserving biodiversity, or ecological integrity and other non-financial criteria.

Any time that an index claims to use qualitative criteria, as opposed to quantitative criteria, it is a clear sign that it is evaluating companies based on subjective criteria. As such, the actual inclusion of the various companies in this index is based on an individual’s or team’s opinions about certain non-financial factors and not necessarily the trailing performance of the stock or the overall size of the company. This is essentially the way that ESG works. The description of the index suggests that WilderHill is only interested in ranking companies based on their environmental criteria, though, not the social or governance aspects that an actual ESG index would typically include. The fact that these criteria were excluded from the website’s description does not necessarily mean that the members of the committee that designed this index are not taking social and governance factors into account when designing this index, but it does not explicitly state that on the index’s website.

How PBW Compares and Differs From Other Clean Energy Funds

The Invesco WilderHill Clean Energy ETF is sponsored by Invesco, which is one of the largest providers of exchange-traded funds in the United States today. The fund has been around for almost as long as the WilderHill Clean Energy Index, which was created in 2004.

PBW is the only exchange-traded fund that aims to track the WilderHill Clean Energy Index. However, there are numerous other exchange-traded funds that invest in the clean energy sector. However, none of them is an especially large fund, as PBW is one of the largest clean energy index funds around, and it only has assets under management of $614.46 million. Here are a few of the other exchange-traded funds that are available to investors who wish to invest in clean energy:

Fund Name

Index Tracked

Virtus Duff & Phelps Clean Energy ETF (VCLN)

The Virtus Duff & Phelps Clean Energy ETF is an actively managed exchange-traded fund. It is not a passive index-tracking fund like the others shown in the chart, and as such, it is not directly comparable. It is also far smaller than the others, with only $5.03 million in assets under management. All of the remaining funds are larger, although the Fidelity Clean Energy ETF has less than $100 million in assets under management.

In addition to the above funds, there are a number of exchange-traded funds that invest in specific segments of the clean energy sector. For example, a fund such as the First Trust Global Wind Energy ETF (FAN) invests specifically in wind power and eschews other types of clean energy. The Invesco WilderHill Clean Energy ETF, in contrast, includes a wider range of companies that include anything from lithium mining to solar power generation. We can see this from looking at the largest positions in the fund’s portfolio:

Lithium mining is a very different business from solar power manufacturing or generation. For example, the profitability of a lithium mining company is largely a function of the price of lithium and the costs to extract the lithium from the ground. Perhaps surprisingly, the price of crude oil is an important factor here because mining companies need to consume crude oil to power their mining equipment. Thus, a rising oil price can have a negative impact on the profits of lithium miners (and vice versa). A solar panel manufacturer is going to be adversely impacted by a rising price of silver due to the fact that a typical solar panel contains between 15 and 20 grams of silver. A company that simply generates solar electricity usually simply sells power to a large buyer under a power purchase agreement, which provides it with relatively stable revenue, although the electricity that it actually produces is dependent on the weather. In short, the companies held in PBW’s portfolio are affected by a variety of different factors, including some that a typical investor might not realize are actual factors. This will have an impact on the fund’s performance, and it is one reason why the performances of the various clean energy exchange-traded funds are different from one another.

We can see here that the various peer funds have wildly different performances in the market:

This chart shows the performance of five of the different clean energy ETFs over the five-year period that ended on October 15, 2025. For the purposes of this chart, I have removed FRNW and VCLN due to the fact that neither of those funds has a performance history for this entire five-year period (their inception dates were after October 15, 2020). Those two are also the smallest of the two funds, which might impact the willingness of investors to choose them. All five of the other peer funds are included in the chart, and we can certainly see stark differences in their performance over this particular period.

In particular, one thing that we notice is that NZAC has been much more stable than the other funds. The majority of these clean energy funds rose substantially in the months following the COVID-19 economic lockdowns, driven by a combination of incredibly low interest rates and expectations that policymakers in Washington, D.C., would look favorably upon these companies. However, renewable energy stocks eventually came under pressure as interest rates rose and threatened the economic viability of many renewable energy projects. NZAC, however, delivered a very different performance, as it never participated in the early 2021 surge, nor did it decline as far as the other funds did once interest rates started to rise. Ultimately, NZAC was the only one of these five exchange-traded funds to have delivered a positive investment return over this particular five-year period. PBW was the worst-performing fund of the peer group over this particular five-year period.

Potential investors are advised to always keep in mind that past performance is no guarantee of future results. While it is certainly true that PBW was the worst-performing fund in its peer group over the five-year period that went from October 15, 2020, through October 15, 2025, that does not mean that it will always be the worst-performing fund out of the group. In fact, over the one-year period that lasted from October 15, 2024, through October 15, 2025, PBW was by far the strongest performer out of its peer group:

This immediately tells us that factors other than regulatory policy and government backing for renewable energy projects have an impact on the performance of renewable energy stocks. After all, starting in mid-January 2025, which was about a fourth of the way into this period, President Trump replaced President Biden as leader of the United States. President Trump’s priorities are generally considered to be less favorable towards renewable energy than President Biden’s were, but the stock prices of the companies that comprise the indices that each of these funds tracks surged beginning in late April 2025, which was well into President Trump’s administration. Admittedly, it is possible that President Trump’s policy of investing the U.S. government’s money in mining companies that he deems critical for national security played a role in this. This could particularly explain why PBW surged during the first year of his administration, as that fund is more heavily invested in lithium miners – particularly Lithium Americas (LAC) – than are the other funds shown in the list. However, this does not fully explain it because ICLN also gained during the first year of the Trump administration, and that fund’s largest position is First Solar (FSLR). Investopedia speculates that the Trump administration’s guidance with respect to clean energy tax credits was less restrictive than investors feared, which helped to drive gains in solar stocks, but the actual reason for the strong performance of renewable energy stocks during the first year of the Trump administration is somewhat unclear.

Clearly, then, PBW’s performance is not going to be identical to the other clean energy ETFs. The fund has very different holdings than its peers, and this changes the overall fundamentals of the underlying portfolio. However, one thing that we do notice fairly consistently is that government policy plays a fairly significant role in the market performance of the companies held by these funds. Many governments around the world, including those in both the United States and Europe, offer things such as tax credits or direct subsidies that can improve the profitability of various projects that these companies frequently undertake. As such, investors are advised to consider the political risks before making an investment in PBW or any other renewable energy fund.

Another thing that investors should consider when purchasing shares of any exchange-traded fund, including PBW, is the liquidity of that fund. It is important that a fund have sufficient liquidity to absorb an investor’s desired trading size, or the price could move adversely against that investor. In the case of PBW, an average of 688,252 shares change hands every day. The share price as of October 15, 2025, is $35.96 per share, so this gives the fund an average of $24,749,542 of average daily trading activity. This should be sufficient to absorb the trades of most retail investors, although institutional investors might want to space their trades out in order to avoid adverse price movements. This chart shows how PBW compares with its peers in terms of liquidity:
